coredata.dc:title: A CdTe/CdS quantum dots amplified graphene quantum dots anodic electrochemiluminescence platform and the application for ascorbic acid detection in fruits
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hongjun
Li, Wang
Zhao, Peng
Nie, Zhou
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description:
Herein, a CdTe/CdS QDs (with a CdTe core and a CdS shell) amplified electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) of nitrogen functional graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) platform is reported. The utilization of CdTe/CdS QDs could facilitate the production of NGQDs radical, and then, a high yield of NGQDs* was formed due to the interaction of NGQDs radical with O2
−, leading to an ∼21-fold ECL enhancement. This ECL enhancing system does not require the addition of coreactant and modification of electrode which is carried out in homogeneous solution. Benefiting from the remarkable ECL enhancement effect, a feasible and sensitive ECL sensor was constructed for ascorbic acid (AA) quantification. The proposed method features a detection limit of 70nM with a linear calibration range from 100nM to 100μM, and exhibits good stability, practicability, and acceptable fabrication reproducibility, showing promising application in real-sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen functional graphene quantum dots
CdTe/CdS quantum dots
Anodic electrochemiluminescence
Ascorbic acid
Fruits

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen functional graphene dots (NGQDs)
Preparation of CdTe/CdS core/ shell QDs
Characterization of nitrogen functional graphene dots (NGQDs)
CdTe/CdS QDs induced anodic ECL enhancement behavior toward NGQDs
Mechanism study for the enhanced anodic ECL of NGQDs
Investigations on the influence of CdTe/CdS QDs concentration, pH and stability
Analytical application of the enhanced anodic ECL system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of NGQDs. Inset: photographs of the NGQDs aqueous solution taken under white light (left) and 365nm UV light (right). (B) HRTEM image of individual NGQDs. (C) Numerical diffractogram from the indicated region. (D) FTIR spectra of GO and NGQDs.

(A) ECL-potential curves of 5μgmL−1 NGQDs in the presence (red line) and absence (black line) of 0.4μgmL−1 CdTe/CdS QDs, and the CdTe/CdS QDs alone (blue line) Inset: the enlarged curves. (B) cyclic votammertry curves of 5μgmL−1 NGQDs in the presence (red line) and absence (black line) of 0.4μgmL−1 CdTe/CdS QDs, and the CdTe/CdS QDs alone (blue line). (C) Proposed pathway for CdTe/CdS QDs enhanced anodic ECL of NGQDs.(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) ECL responses of the 5μgmL−1 NGQDs before (black line) and after (red line) being bubbled with nitrogen. (B) ECL responses of the 5μgmL−1 NGQDs with 0.4μgmL−1 CdTe/CdS QDs before (black line) and after (red line) being bubbled with nitrogen and exposed to air (blue line). (C) ECL responses of 5μgmL−1 NGQDs before (black line) and after (red line) the addition of 24μgmL−1 SOD. (D) ECL responses of 5μgmL−1 NGQDs with 0.4μgmL−1 CdTe/CdS QDs before (black line) and after (red line) the addition of 24μgmL−1 SOD.(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Photoluminescence spectra of NGQDs (blue line) and ECL (red line) spectra of NGQDs with CdTe/CdS QDs. The excitation wavelength of PL spectrum is 365nm, and the ECL data were collected at +1.24V. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of NGQDs (a) and CdTe/CdS QDs (b) and normalized PL spectra of NGQDs (c) and CdTe/CdS QDs (d).

(A) ECL responses of NGQDs by injecting different amount of CdTe/CdS QDs. (B) ECL-potential curves of NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs under different pH value. (C) ECL responses of CdTe/CdS QDs enhanced NGQDs based sensor toward different AA concentration. Inset: calibration curve. (D) ECL response after addition of different concentration interferents which is 1.0mM of citric acid (CA), fructose (Fru), sorbitol (Sorb), sucrose (Sue), glucose (Glu), Vitamin B1 (Vb1) respectively, 500μM of Vitamin B2 (Vb2), the mixture and 100μM of ascorbic acid (AA) in air-saturated pH 8.0 PBS.
Determination of ascorbic acid in various real samples.

head.ce:title: A CdTe/CdS quantum dots amplified graphene quantum dots anodic electrochemiluminescence platform and the application for ascorbic acid detection in fruits

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University, Changsha, 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Herein, a CdTe/CdS QDs (with a CdTe core and a CdS shell) amplified electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL) of nitrogen functional graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) platform is reported. The utilization of CdTe/CdS QDs could facilitate the production of NGQDs radical, and then, a high yield of NGQDs* was formed due to the interaction of NGQDs radical with O2
−, leading to an ∼21-fold ECL enhancement. This ECL enhancing system does not require the addition of coreactant and modification of electrode which is carried out in homogeneous solution. Benefiting from the remarkable ECL enhancement effect, a feasible and sensitive ECL sensor was constructed for ascorbic acid (AA) quantification. The proposed method features a detection limit of 70nM with a linear calibration range from 100nM to 100μM, and exhibits good stability, practicability, and acceptable fabrication reproducibility, showing promising application in real-sample.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs), a kind of electrochemiluminescence (ECL) emitter, owning several distinctive merits such as size controlled luminescence and excellent photostability compared with conventional molecular emitters, have accordingly been widely applied for ECL sensing [1–3]. Recently, QDs based ECL sensors with relevant properties, since reported by Ding and Bard [4], are currently extensively constructed applying commonly used coreactants [5–7]. Although extrinsic coreactants have been employed to enhance QDs based ECL intensity, it is sometimes hard to satisfy the requirement of sensitive detection for the trace and ultratrace analyte. Currently, varieties of nano-materials, such as graphene oxide (GO), AuNPs and CdSeTe@ZnS-SiO2 QDs, are applied to enhance the intensity of QDs based ECL system even with the existence of coreactants [6,8–11]. Some of them, elaborative design for the electrode surface [6] might make the sensor fabrication tedious, and the addition of coreactants such as peroxydisulfate [11] and H2O2 might encounter with the dilemma of destruction of the buffer circumstance and analysts, limiting its application when incorporated into biomolecule because peroxydisulfate and H2O2 are a kind of oxidant. Confronting these drawbacks, to achieve a simple and benign ECL approach, therefore, the development of an electrolysis-friendly strategy without the modification of electrode is full of significance.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a type of carbon-based material, has some outstanding merits, including chemical inertness, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and excellent photostability, thus, endowing them perfect potential applications in chemical sensors and biosensors. Recently, studies for elucidating the properties of GQDs, including photoluminescence [12] and electro-generated ECL [13,14], are reported. GQDs sensors based on its photoluminescence properties are fabricated for kinds of targets [13,15,16], and GQDs based ECL sensors with relevant properties are currently applied for determination of ATP and heavy metal ions [14,17]. Currently, nitrogen functionalized GQDs (NGQDs), a kind of GQDs analogous, gains attentions due to the capabilities of changing the electronic characteristics that derived from functionalization of nitrogen. In light of its optic properties, investigations are just concerning on the photoluminescence studies [18–20], endowing an opportunity to study the ECL emissive NGQDs and elucidate its relative ECL properties.

In the present work, water-soluble NGQDs that can generate anodic ECL at low onset potential (+0.6V vs. Ag/AgCl) is prepared and the relative ECL properties are reported. More importantly, we proposed a CdTe/CdS QDs amplified ECL platform where CdTe/CdS QDs facilitated the generation of NGQDs radicals and formed a high yield of NGQDs*, then leading to an ∼21 times enhancement of ECL intensity compared with the system without CdTe/CdS QDs. Inspired by the peculiar discovery, with the advantage of low onset potential and free of electrode modification, a feasible ECL sensing approach for ascorbic acid (AA) detection is proposed and used in fruit application. In summary, this work will provide solid foundation for the further understanding of QDs induced ECL enhancement phenomenon for the other QDs and also pave an alternative way for QDs based sensitive ECL sensor construction.

Graphite powder (99.9%, 325 mesh) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. CdCl2, NaBH4, Na2TeO3 were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Superoxide dismutase (SOD) and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 99%) were gained from Sigma-Aldrich. Phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) were prepared using 0.2M KH2PO4 and Na2HPO4. All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q system (18.2MΩ·cm) was used in all runs.

The electrochemical and ECL measurements were carried out on an MPI-A multifunctional electrochemical and chemiluminescent analytical system (Remex, Xi'an, China) at room temperature with a configuration consisting of an indium/tin oxide (ITO) working electrode, a platinum counter electrode, and a Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl solution) reference electrode. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a Tecnai G2 F30 ST instruments (FEI Co., U.S.A.) operating at 300kV. Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a 1cm path length quartz cuvette on a Hitachi F-4500 spectrometer. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained on Beckman Coulter DU-800. FT-IR spectra were recorded on Nicolet 400 FT-IR spectrometer (Madison, WI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen functional graphene dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs were prepared by reduction of nitrogen functionalized graphene oxide quantum dots (NGOQDs). Briefly, NGOQDs were initially prepared according to the reported method [18] and were prepared at concentration of 1mgmL−1. Then, 10mL of the as prepared homogeneous dispersion substance and 200μL of hydrazine were mixed and transferred into Teflon-lined autoclave (25mL) and heated at 180°C for 12h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitated insoluble large fragments were filtered out using 0.45μm millipore membrane and the desired supernatant NGQDs were collected. The brown supernatant was dispersed in 100mL ethanol using rotary evaporation to vaporise excessive hydrazine and then freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CdTe/CdS core/ shell QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CdTe/CdS QDs were prepared according to previously reported method [21]. A typical synthesis is described as follows: First, 74μL MPA and 20mg trisodium citrate were added to 100mL CdCl2 solution (0.5 mmol) in succession and stirred for 10min. After that, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 11.2 by adding 0.1M NaOH. Then, 4.4mg Na2TeO3 and 20mg NaBH4 were injected into the mixture under rapid stirring obtaining a total molar ratio of Cd2+/MPA/TeO3 2− fixed at 1:1.7:0.4. Finally, the reaction mixture was heated at 90°C for 8h. The samples were carefully purified coupling with a subsequent ultrafiltration with a centrifugation at 8000rpm to collect the fraction with a nominal molecular weight of > 3kDa before subsequent measurement.

The ECL emissions were detected using an MPI-A multifunctional electrochemical and chemiluminescent analytical system. The emission window was placed in front of the photomultiplier tube set at 600V. The typical testing solutions were fixed to 400μL with 0.2M PBS (pH 8.0) containing 5μg mL−1 NGQDs and 0.4μgmL−1 CdTe/CdS QDs solution. The ECL spectrum was measured by collecting the ECL data at +1.24V during cyclic potential sweep with twelve pieces of long-pass filters at cutoff wavelengths of 340, 360, 380, 400, 420, 450, 470, 490, 550, 580, 630 and 650nm, and the data were fitted in accordance with reported literature [22].

The AA content in fruit samples were extracted using the following procedure. Briefly, two-grams of fruit samples were randomly taken and pounded in centrifugal tube. Then 10mL of ultrapure water was added into the tube, mechanically shaked for 1h and centrifugated at 8,000rpm for 15min. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.45μm millipore filter and collected for immediate assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nitrogen functional graphene dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The water-soluble NGQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal approach [18]. In particular, the present NGQDs can be freely dispersed in water without further ultrasonic dispersion. The solution of NGQDs with a deep yellow appearance emits light blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 1 A inset). The morphologies of the obtained NGQDs characterized by TEM show that the resultant NGQDs own diameters of less than 10nm, and are rarely aggregated suggesting its excellent dispersion ability (Fig. 1A). In addition, the lattice fringes of the NGQDs can be clearly observed from high-resolution TEM image, exhibiting a high crystalline structure with an in-plane lattice spacing of ∼0.267nm (Fig. 1B), which is comparable to that of graphene prepared by other methods [23]. Furthermore, the fast Fourier transform of the selected area (in Fig. 1C) reveals the hexagonal crystalline structure which is consistent with single layered graphene [24]. The descriptions above reveal the preparation of water dispersed and single-layered NGQDs. Afterwards, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy was used to investigate the bonding composition of the NGQDs and its functional groups (Fig. 1D). Disappearance about the peak of 1727cm−1 and appearance about the peaks of 1380 and 3134 cm−1 (the vibration of C–N and N–H bonds [14,18] suggest the satisfied preparation of NGQDs. In addition, bathochromic shift of the fluorescence emission position of the NGQDs emerges as the excitation wavelength increases (data are not shown), which is in accordance with other reported graphene quantum dots [23] further proving the preparation of NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CdTe/CdS QDs induced anodic ECL enhancement behavior toward NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Aiming to exhibit its potential on ECL sensor construction, the ECL properties of the as-prepared NGQDs were investigated. When the potential was cycled positively between 0 and 1.25V, NGQDs exhibit an intense ECL emission at 1.24V with an onset potential at 0.6V (the black line, inset of Fig. 2 A), and generate two irreversible redox peaks (the black line, Fig. 2B) ascribing to the loss of electrons of the modified amine groups which is similar to the previously phenomena about electrochemical oxidation of aliphatic amines [25]. The emissive onset potential is lower than other reported anodic quantum dots, such as CdTe/CdS [26] and CdTe QDs [27], being conductive to fabricate voltaic sensitive ELC sensor. Surprisingly, upon adding CdTe/CdS QDs (Fig. S1), the ECL intensity is greatly improved (the red line, Fig. 2A), which is about 21-fold of that treated without CdTe/CdS QDs. The phenomenon is graphically represented in Fig. 2C. It is inspiring that discovering QDs can enhance the ECL of the other QDs in homogeneous solution without any modification on electrode endowing QDs based ECL system with improved sensitivity and operational versatility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism study for the enhanced anodic ECL of NGQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To clarify the concrete ECL processes, we investigated the effects of O2 and O2
− on the ECL generation of CdTe/CdS QDs enhanced NGQDs system. As depicted in Fig. 3 A and B, once the emission solution was bubbled with nitrogen, violent ECL intensity decrease observes (the red line) both in the solution containing NGQDs alone and in NGQDs and CdTe/CdS QDs coexistence (donated as NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs), indicating that dissolved oxygen is able to involve in the CdTe/CdS QDs enhanced NGQDs ECL emission process. Besides, upon the nitrogen saturated NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs solution exposing to air again with concomitant increasing in dissolving oxygen, the ECL intensity recovers (the blue line in Fig. 3B) further supporting the participation of oxygen. SOD, a typical O2
− capture, was added into the air-saturated ECL solution to explore the existence of O2
− during this anodic ECL procedure. As shown in Fig. 3C and D, attributing to efficient scavenger of O2
−, the ECL intensity decreases a large extent both in the solution containing NGQDs alone and the NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs solution, indicating that O2
− as key species virtually participates in the ECL emission of CdTe/CdS QDs enhanced NGQDs ECL process. The obtained phenomena about effects of O2 and O2
− on the ECL intensity of NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs are consistent with that of NGQDs alone. Consequently, we believe that the dissolved oxygen involves in the CdTe/CdS QDs enhanced NGQDs ECL emission process as the species of O2
−. Notably, the air-saturated ECL buffer was bubbled with enough oxygen (Fig. S2), a bit of enhancing action is observed indicating that the dissolved oxygen is sufficient for producing stable ECL in common air-saturated solution.

Considering the striking enhancement phenomenon that involves two different quantum dots in the ECL system, including both NGQDs and CdTe/CdS QDs, studies are necessary to distinguish the real luminophor with an attempt on revealing the ECL processes. To this end, the relationships between the ECL of NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs mixture and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of NGQDs and CdTe/CdS QDs alone were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the emission peak of ECL spectrum of NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs mixture at 420nm approximates to the PL emission of NGQDs at 440nm (excited at 365nm) but distinguishes from the PL emission of CdTe/CdS QDs at 630nm, revealing that the anodic ECL of NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs mixture originates from NGQDs and is assisted by CdTe/CdS QDs. Besides, negligible ECL emission is obtained with CdTe/CdS QDs alone under the working potential (Fig. S3), suggesting that the enhancement is not coming from the intrinsic ECL of CdTe/CdS QDs as well.

Regarding to the enhancement phenomenon, according to previous reports, two main possible enhancement factors, energy transfer effects and intermediate for electron transfer, might contribute to the quantum dots induced ECL enhancement [9,16]. The intermediate mechanism might play the dominant actions because the UV absorption of NGQDs does not overlap with PL emission of CdTe/CdS (Fig. 4B), namely the energy transfer process that account for the ECL enhancement effects is not happened [9]. To discuss the possibility of the participation of intermediate, the band energy levels (including the HOMO and the LUMO) come into sight on account of its important role in electronic transferring process of quantum dots during electrochemical reactions and irradiations. It is reported that electron can be injected into the lower LUMO of quantum dots [28–31]. Actually, the LUMO of CdTe/CdS QDs (−3.62eV) is located between the LUMO of NGQDs (−2.19eV) and that of the ITO (−4.7eV) electrode [32] (detailed information for LUMO calculation is presented in Fig. S4). Encouraged by these facts, therefore, it is reasonable to speculate that CdTe/CdS QDs might be a good intermediate for electron transfer from NGQDs to ITO electrode. Namely, NGQDs oxidation is facilitated by CdTe/CdS QDs [9].

In light of CdTe/CdS QDs acting as intermediate accelerating electron transfer of NGQDs, analogous to anodic ECL pathways of other quantum dots [9,27] in the presence of oxygen, the reasonable electrochemical reactions of NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs mixture and O2 on the ITO electrode are summarized as the following equations (1)∼(4):
(1)
N G Q D s +
I n S O
x
→
C d e / C d s
N G Q D s (
h +
) +
I n S O
x
(
e −
)
(2)
O
2
+
I n S O
x
(
e −
) →
O
2
−
+
I n S O
x
(3)
N G Q D s (
h
+
) +
O
2
−
→
N G Q D s
*
+
O
2
(4)
N G Q D s
*
→ N G Q D s + h v
Briefly, NGQDs oxidation is facilitated by CdTe/CdS QDs accelerating the formation of NGQDs(h+), O2 traps hot electrons from ITO electrode to generate O2
−. Then, the improved NGQDs(h+) and O2
− combines each other harvesting a high yield of NGQDs* that finally emitted light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigations on the influence of CdTe/CdS QDs concentration, pH and stability
ce:section.ce:para:
According to the intermediate involvement properties, the amount of CdTe/CdS QDs suggests to be tightly related to the enhancement effect. Therefore, different amounts of CdTe/CdS QDs were added into NGQDs solution to find out the optimal concentration (Fig. 5 A). The ECL enhancing action increases with the increasing concentration of CdTe/CdS QDs and comes to a plateau after 0.4μgmL−1. Consequently, the concentration of CdTe/CdS QDs was selected to 0.4μgmL−1 and used throughout. Generally, operational stability is crucial for practical application of the ECL system. To this end, electrolyte pH value that is imperative to the stability and intensity of ECL system was investigated to make the ECL system consummates (Fig. 5B). The ECL emission was unobservable when the pH was lower than 5.0. With the increasing pH from 5.0 to 9.0, the ECL intensities increase while become unstable with the pH value beyond 8.0. Two main factors are contributed to the ECL increasing results. Firstly, with the increasing pH, the electron transfer from the conduction band of ITO to O2 was easier [33,27]. Secondly, the resulting O2
− species during the ECL process were more stable at high pH, was favorable to the ECL emission and led to the increasing ECL intensity [27]. Therefore, pH 8.0 was chosen as the optimum implementing condition in following experiments. Besides, eight continuous potential cycle scans were carried out under the optimized condition. Strong and stable ECL signals were observed with a relative standard deviation of 3.6% (Fig. S5), revealing the favorable stability of the ECL signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of the enhanced anodic ECL system
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the satisfactory ECL stability and operational simplicity of CdTe/CdS QDs enhanced NGQDs ECL system, and the unique ECL enhancement discovery, a feasible ECL sensing approach was constructed for AA detection. AA plays an important role in body health as it is necessary for the formation of collagen, assists in the absorption of iron and lack of it provokes increased susceptibility to many kinds of infections [34,35]. Therefore, development of the analytical methods for AA is of great significance. Currently, AA identified as the radical scavenger, has been reported able to quench the quantum dots based ECL emission system [36]. In this work, the ECL intensity of NGQDs@CdTe/CdS QDs system can be effectively quenched by trace amounts of AA, demonstrating its promising potential for AA determination. As shown in Fig. 5C, the ECL intensity decreases with the increasing of AA concentration under the optimum condition. Taking the relative intensity change of I0–I as the function of the concentration of AA, where I0 was the initial intensity and I was the ECL intensity after adding AA, the (I0–I)/I0 was linearly depended on the logarithm of AA concentration in the range of 100nM to 100μM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.992 (the inset of Fig. 5C). The linear regression equation was calculated as (I0–I)/I0 =0.311log[AA] - 0.574 with a limit of detection (LOD) being 70nM (S/N=3). In addition, owning to the ECL enhancement impact of CdTe/CdS QDs, the LOD is superior to the results obtained under the existence of NGQDs alone (Fig. S6), indicating a sensitive quantitative behavior. In particular, the achieved LOD is similar to traditional ECL based AA sensors that demand complicated electrode modification [36], and is even lower than some of the methods that is tedious in modification or dependent on expensive equipment, such as electrochemical method [37,38] and high-performance liquid chromatography [39]. Combining the advantages of modification free with low detection limit, the proposed ECL system suggests promising potential for simple and feasible AA sensing.

Generally, selectivity is critical for practical application of the ECL system. The selectivity of the ECL sensing system was also investigated by means of measuring the ECL responses upon adding other shared contents. As shown in Fig. 5D, 100μM of AA can almost quench the ECL of the system, while 10 times of the other contents such as citric acid (CA), fructose (Fru), sorbitol (Sorb), sucrose (Sue), glucose (Glu), vitamin B1 (Vb1) and 5 times of vitamin B2 (Vb2) and even their mixture only have a slight interference, demonstrating its perfect anti-interference ability and possibility for AA sensing.

The concentrations of AA in three kinds of samples, such as grapefruit, orange and sweet orange were tested with the proposed ECL system. The contents of each sample calculated form the calibration curve were compared with those obtained using the AA detection kit method, and the satisfactory data in Table 1 further validate the feasibility and accuracy of the proposed ECL system. Furthermore, the relative standard deviation (RSD) for six parallel experiments by 50μM AA addition was 4.0%, indicating high reproducibility and stability using the electrode without any modification. In summary, the proposed ECL system AA sensor has high selectivity, stability and good reproducibility which guarantee its application in practical sample.

In conclusion, we have developed an ECL pathway of NGQDs with the participation of CdTe/CdS QDs. CdTe/CdS QDs can facilitate the oxidation of NGQDs and accelerate the output of NGQDs radical, which can improve the emission efficiency and result in 21-fold enhancement on ECL intensities. The QDs amplified ECL platform is carried out in homogeneous solution with dissolved oxygen involved in the ECL process, possessing merits of free of electrode modification and avoiding the use of the adverse oxidant such as K2S2O8 and H2O2. We used the as-prepared ECL system for ascorbic acid sensing with high selectivity, stability, good reproducibility and further used it for AA detection in fruits sample. This work improves the QDs ECL technology, provides evident proof to understand the QDs enhanced ECL phenomenon, and supplies an alternative way for sensitive ECL sensor construction.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21222507, 21305037, 21475037, and 21235002), the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program, No. 2011CB911002), the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of NSFC (Grant 21221003), the Excellent Youth Foundation of Hunan Scientific Committee(No.2015JJ1005), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2015.08.015.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fundamentals and bioanalytical applications of functional quantum dots as electrogenerated emitters of chemiluminescence
Generated versus photoexcited luminescence from semiconductor nanomaterials: bridging the valley between two worlds
Electrochemistry and electrogenerated chemiluminescence from silicon nanocrystal quantum dots
Design and biosensing of Mg2+-dependent DNAzyme–triggered patiometric electrochemiluminescence

Supersensitive detection of chlorinated phenols by multiple amplification electrochemiluminescence sensing based on carbon quantum dots/graphene
Revealing carbon nanodots as coreactants of the anodic electrochemiluminescence of Ru(bpy)3 2+
Graphene oxide amplified electrogenerated chemiluminescence of quantum dots and its selective sensing for glutathione from thiol-containing compounds
Electrochemiluminescence signal amplification combined with a conformation-switched hairpin DNA probe for determining the methylation level and positioning the Hsp53 tumor suppressor gene
Common origin of green luminescence in carbon nanodots and graphene quantum dots
Graphene quantum dots/L–cysteine coreactant electrochemiluminescence system and its application in sensing lead(II) ions
A facile microwave avenue to electrochemiluminescent two-color graphene quantum dots
Graphene quantum dots sensor for the determination of graphene oxide in environmental water samples
A novel composite of graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol
Electrochemiluminescence of blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots and its application in ultrasensitive aptasensor for adenosine triphosphate detection
Eco–friendly synthesis of size-controllable amine-functionalized graphene quantum dots with antimycoplasma properties
Deep ultraviolet to near-Infra red emission and photoresponse in layered N–doped graphene quantum dots
Optically tunable amino–functionalized graphene quantum dots
Aqueous one-pot synthesis of bright and ultrasmall CdTe/CdS near–infrared–emitting quantum dots and their application for tumor targeting in vivo

Versatile electrochemiluminescent biosensor for protein-nucleic acid interaction based on the unique quenching effect of deoxyguanosine–5′–phosphate on electrochemiluminescence of CdTe/ZnS quantum dots
Evidence of nanocrystalline semiconducting graphene monoxide during thermal reduction of graphene oxide in vacuum
Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue–luminescent graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical oxidation of aliphatic amines and their attachment to carbon and metal surfaces
Anodic near-infrared electrochemiluminescence from CdTe/CdS coresmall/shellthick quantum dots and their sensing ability of Cu2+
Anodic electrochemiluminescence of CdTe quantum dots and its energy transfer for detection of catechol derivatives
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
Breakthroughs in the development of semiconductor–sensitized solar cells
Core-shell heterojunction of silicon nanowire arrays and carbon quantum dots for photovoltaic devices and self-driven photodetectors
